title: DAVID — Alfred the Great: A King in His Own English
logline: Biography of Alfred the Great with his own attested Old English from the Pastoral Care preface; sources on file.

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Royal Tongues — dead languages and the voices of history
brand.cta: Follow the Archive — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

sources.citation: Alfred, Preface to the Old English translation of Gregory's Pastoral Care (Hierdeboc, c. 890) — Alfred's own words on learning and translation
sources.notes: Royal prose attributed to Alfred himself; the opening greeting is the attested period line

sources.citation: Asser, Vita Ælfredi regis Angul Saxonum (893) — contemporary Latin biography of Alfred
sources.notes: Eyewitness Welsh bishop at Alfred's court

sources.citation: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle (compiled under Alfred, late 9th c.) — reign, Viking wars, Treaty of Wedmore

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Tonight, Alfred the Great — the only English king ever called Great, and one of the very few medieval rulers whose own words we can still read.

Born around eight forty-nine, king of Wessex, he held off the Viking Great Army, struck a treaty that split England, and then did something stranger — he sat down to translate Latin books into English himself.
His court ran on two tongues: Latin for the church and scholarship, and Old English — West Saxon — for law, chronicle, and the books he wanted every freeborn youth to read.
Ælfred kyning hateð gretan Wærferð biscep his wordum luflice ond freondlice — Alfred the king sends his greetings to Bishop Wærferth, lovingly and as a friend — the king's own attested Old English.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED · Old English · Pastoral Care preface

beats.speech_text: He died in eight ninety-nine. Most kings reach us through others' chronicles; Alfred reaches us in the first person — proof that a crown could also hold a pen.

lines: PERIOD LINE: Attested Old English — Alfred's own preface to the Pastoral Care
METHOD: Royal prose (Alfred) + Asser's Life + Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
